issues.title: Guidance on how to avoid encrypting PQ(/T) and traditional for the same user
issues.author: wussler
issues.body:
The text contains the following statement:
> Implementations MUST NOT encrypt a message to a purely traditional public-key encryption key of a recipient if it is encrypted to a PQ/T key of the same recipient.

This should be expanded to ensure what to check when encrypting to the same recipient. Should it be the primary identity?

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
Meeting 2023-01-19:
* Aron: was difficult to implement. At which level should this be done: application level or library level? Check for different keys how / where? use primary identity as identification ?
* Andreas: should at least inform the user
* Aron: then not "MUST". In go we cannot model warnings, only errors
* Andreas: when allowing users to use some primary identity ...
* Aron: concept of linked keys with same primary identity, an impl. should only encrypt to one of these. Could be seen as task for application or for library.
* Aron: keep statement but give guidance for how to do it. Define standard behaviour
* Andreas: Aron, would you come up with a proposal?
* Aron: yes, but not clear so far. Key pointing to another key as newer then no false positive.
* Stavros: is parallel public key encryption done often?
* Aron: can be done by the library if it receives the key information
* Stavros: migration sec says prefer PQC
* Aron: but not MUST, but if using PQC encryption "use only PQC"
* Stavros: talking about the same certificate:
* Aron: not necessarily. Cannot safely decide whether 2 certs belong to the same user. thus false positives can happen. Should not stop encryption.
* Stavros: 2 statements about this: this one and 7.1 migration considerations. There: SHOULD prefer PQC certificate.
* Aron: reference to this statement in migrations considerations.
* Falko: make this statmt a SHOULD?
* Aron: keep it, but give guidance
* Stavros: can always make PQC cert
* Falko: but what if no end date in old
* Aron: will make proposal

issues.title: Missing key generation procedures
issues.author: wussler
issues.body: Encryption, decryption, signing and verification are described, but not how to generate keys

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body: I think this is perfectly in line with the existing OpenPGP standards. As far as I know, they do not specify anything about key generation. Historically, the OpenPGP standard is a mere "on wire format", meaning that it only specifies how to encode the data on the wire and not how implementations are supposed to process the data. I think latest with the crypto-refresh that is changing in the sense that more specifications about the behaviour of an implementation are made. But I think key generation is still out of scope for the OpenPGP standard. The NIST standards will specify how this is done.

comments.author: wussler
comments.body:
AFAIK our draft is much more verbose with the encryption and decryption procedures, while RFC 4880, 6637, or the crypto-refresh tend to be more dry about that.
My point is that if we include verbose and explicit instructions on how to encrypt and decrypt, we should also include a paragraph stating that keys should be generated natively according to \[spec\].

I'd propose something like:
```
### Key generation procedure {#ecc-kyber-generation}

To generate Kyber + ECC composite key the implementation MUST independently
generate  the Kyber and the ECC component keys.
For Kyber this is done in native format as specified in {{Kyber-Subm}} and encoded
as fixed-length octet strings depending on the security level. 
For ECC this is done following the relative specification in {{RFC7748}}, 
{{NIST-SP800-186}}, or {{RFC5639}}, and encoding the outputs as fixed-length 
octet strings based on the curve's field size.
```

Similar for Dilithium and SPHINCS+.

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
Minutes Meeting 2023-01-19:

* Aron: crypto-refresh already addesses key generation of X...
* Stavros: should have paragraph
* Falko: agreed

issues.title: Various review comments
issues.author: falko-strenzke
issues.body:
~~Regarding the sentence in the introduction:~~

~~> "Here, the term "composite" indicates that the combination of the two components forms a single atomic object.~~

~~I don't think that this sentence is saying something useful. Clearly, they are not essentially atomic (as nothing is in the known universe, not even atoms). What is meant here, is that they appear as single cryptogrpaphic schemes on the protocol layer. Accordingly, that is what I would propose to state here.~~

Falko: moved to separate issue

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
~~Section 4.1.1.1:~~

~~> Generate an ephemeral key pair {v, V=vG}, without any masking or clamping as defined in [[RFC7748](file:///home/fstrenzke/doc/pqc_thunderbird/draft-openpgp-pqc/draft-wussler-openpgp-pqc.html#RFC7748)]~~

~~I think this can be understood in two ways:~~
~~1. that the definition in RFC7748 should be used and that this definition does not include "masking or clamping"~~
~~2. that "masking or clamping" should not be used, and that "masking and clamping" is defined in RFC7748~~

~~In my understanding, the first variant is the intended statement. To make that entirely clear, I would rather write: "Generate an ephemeral key pair {v, V=vG} according to the procedure defined in RFC7748, that is, without any measure such as masking or clamping that are applied previous OpenPGP specifications of this scheme."~~

PR with the proposed change is in progess...

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
~~In Section 4.1.2 we state~~

~~> Extract the component public key kyberPublicKey that is part of the recipient's composite public key~~

~~This seems inconsistent in wording with the descriptions for the EC-KEMs, where no mentioning of the extraction of the component public key from the composite key is made. IMO it should be mentioned in for each scheme or for none.~~

Falko:  moved to separate issue

comments.author: wussler
comments.body:
~~> Section 4.1.1.1~~
~~> the first variant is the intended statement~~

~~Correct. The spec still defines clamping, but not in the key generation context.~~

~~> Generate an ephemeral key pair {v, V=vG} according to the procedure defined in RFC7748, that is, without any measure such as masking or clamping that are applied previous OpenPGP specifications of this scheme.~~

~~I like it! Mind making a PR?~~

Falko: strikeout as this point is covered in an upcoming PR

comments.author: wussler
comments.body:
~~> In Section 4.1.2~~
~~>This seems inconsistent in wording with the descriptions for the EC-KEMs, ~~

~~In the ECC sections we mention~~
```...where R is the component public key eccPublicKey...```
~~I would consider removing point (1) from 4.1.2 and rewrite~~
``` 
2. Invoke (kyberCipherText, keyShare) <- kyberKem.encap(kyberPublicKey) 
   where kyberPublicKey is extracted from recipient's composite public key
```

Falko: moved to separate issue

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
~~In Section 4.2.3 we have a forward reference to Section 4.3.2. I think normally in a specification one would only make backwards references to things already defined. Thus the referenced section specifying the packet structure should better be moved in front IMO.~~

~~(Section 4.2.4 also has a similar forward reference)~~

Falko: moved to separate issue

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
~~In Section 5.2.1 it says: ~~
~~> Given that Dilithium + ECC signature support is mandatory, an implementation MUST support SHA3-256 and SHOULD support SHA3-512.~~

~~The logic implied by "Given ..." is not entirely convincing to me, as it sounds as that the requirements stated the second clause sentence somehow naturally arise based on the first clause. But that seems not to hold, as an implementation could also just support one of them. So I would suggest to simply remove the first clause of that sentence, i.e. let the sentence start with "An implementations MUST ..."~~

Resolved already.

comments.author: wussler
comments.body:
~~> forward reference~~

~~I personally don't have anything against forward references. I usually like even the backrefs, so that you can navigate the spec in both directions.
Generally, when implementing, I don't use the spec linearly~~

Falko: moved to separate issue

comments.author: wussler
comments.body:
~~> as an implementation could also just support one of them~~

~~Indeed. But here we define which one of the two is "more important".~~

~~We can rephrase it as~~
```
~~An implementation MUST support SHA3-256 and SHOULD support SHA3-512, since 
this specification mandates Dilithium + ECC signature support.~~
```

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
~~> > as an implementation could also just support one of them~~
>
~~> Indeed. But here we define which one of the two is "more important".~~
> 
~~> We can rephrase it as~~
> 
> ```
> An implementation MUST support SHA3-256 and SHOULD support SHA3-512, since 
> this specification mandates Dilithium + ECC signature support.
> ```

~~I don't think that this rephrasing makes an essential difference. But it is also not a really severe point in my view, so maybe we simply leave it as it is.~~

comments.author: wussler
comments.body:
~~I personally like it more, as it puts the focus on the MUST. We can also drop the "since..." but I would keep some justification for that must there.~~

~~What about~~
```
An implementation MUST support SHA3-256 and SHOULD support SHA3-512, in
order to support the hash binding with Dilithium + ECC signatures.
```

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
~~> I personally like it more, as it puts the focus on the MUST. We can also drop the "since..." but I would keep some justification for that must there.~~
> 
~~> What about~~
> 
> ```
> An implementation MUST support SHA3-256 and SHOULD support SHA3-512, in
> order to support the hash binding with Dilithium + ECC signatures.
> ```

~~Would also be OK for me.~~

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
~~Section 8.4:~~

~~> In order not to extend the attack surface, we bind the hash algorithm to the hash internally used into the signature algorithm.~~

~~I don't understand the meaning of the word "into" here. What is supposed to signify exactly? In my understanding, we are ensuring that the hash algorithm used for message digestion is the same as the one used internally in the signature algorithm.~~

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body: ~~In the Security Considerations, under Section 8.5, a normative statement is made ("MUST NOT"). In my understanding, normative statements do not belong in the Security Considerations, but under one of the normative sections.~~

comments.author: wussler
comments.body:
~~This is technically already in the crypto-refresh: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-openpgp-crypto-refresh-07.html#section-12.2.3~~

~~I would like to reiterate here in the security considerations~~

Falko: moved to separate issue (applies also to comment above this one)

comments.author: wussler
comments.body:
~~> Section 8.4:~~
> 
~~> > In order not to extend the attack surface, we bind the hash algorithm to the hash internally used into the signature algorithm.~~
> 
~~> I don't understand the meaning of the word "into" here. What is supposed to signify exactly? In my understanding, we are ensuring that the hash algorithm used for message digestion is the same as the one used internally in the signature algorithm.~~

~~We can do `in` instead~~

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
~~In Section 5.2.2 and 5.2.3 there are statements like~~
~~> Sign dataDigest with eddsa.sign() from [Section 5.1.1]~~

~~I think it is somewhat unlucky to speak of "signing" here, because word this means "create a signature and add it to the signed data". However, how to add the signature to the packet is not specified here. This becomes clear only later when the signature packet structure is explained. ~~

~~I suggest to change the specification to saying "create an EdDSA signature with ..." and "create a Dilithium signature with ..." and then in a new step after these two: "encode the EdDSA and Dilithium signatures according to the the packet structure given in Section ...". But sure there also other possibilities to make the same statement.~~

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
~~> > Section 8.4:~~
> > > In order not to extend the attack surface, we bind the hash algorithm to the hash internally used into the signature algorithm.~~
> > 
> > 
~~> > I don't understand the meaning of the word "into" here. What is supposed to signify exactly? In my understanding, we are ensuring that the hash algorithm used for message digestion is the same as the one used internally in the signature algorithm.~~

Add some verified test data to the RFC for:

issues.title: definition of term "composite"
issues.author: falko-strenzke
issues.body:
Regarding the sentence in the introduction:

> "Here, the term "composite" indicates that the combination of the two components forms a single atomic object.

I don't think that this sentence is saying something useful. Clearly, they are not essentially atomic (as nothing is in the known universe, not even atoms). What is meant here, is that they appear as single cryptogrpaphic schemes on the protocol layer. Accordingly, that is what I would propose to state here.

comments.author: TJ-91
comments.body:
> they appear as single cryptogrpaphic schemes on the protocol layer

I think this was the original statement and we replaced it.
The problem I raised was that "protocol layer" is not clear terminology. In internal discussions we distinguish protocol and algorithm layer but in the draft everything is the protocol layer.

Perhaps we can just phrase it slightly differently, for example that they appear as single cryptographic schemes to the rest of the protocol.

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body: If the word "layer" is the problem, then I propose just to leave it out and say:  "they appear as single cryptographic schemes in the protocol".

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body: Addressed in https://github.com/openpgp-pqc/draft-openpgp-pqc/pull/22

issues.title: should we use forward reference?
issues.author: falko-strenzke
issues.body:
# Falko's original remark

In Section 4.2.3 we have a forward reference to Section 4.3.2. I think normally in a specification one would only make backwards references to things already defined. Thus the referenced section specifying the packet structure should better be moved in front IMO.

(Section 4.2.4 also has a similar forward reference)

# Comment on this from Aron (from previous issue):

> forward reference

I personally don't have anything against forward references. I usually like even the backrefs, so that you can navigate the spec in both directions.
Generally, when implementing, I don't use the spec linearly

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
Meeting 2023-01-19: 

* Aron: circle reference not avoidable for data generation and their encoding
* Stavros: do we have many forward references?
* Aron: a couple in the context of packet formats
* Falko: agreed to the circular dependency problem
* All: generally we should make it without forward references
* Stavros: will read through and check for forward references again

issues.title: Should we have normative statements in the Security Considerations?
issues.author: falko-strenzke
issues.body:
# Falko's original remark

In the Security Considerations, under Section 8.5, a normative statement is made ("MUST NOT"). In my understanding, normative statements do not belong in the Security Considerations, but under one of the normative sections.

# Comment by Aron (from previous issue)

This is technically already in the crypto-refresh: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-openpgp-crypto-refresh-07.html#section-12.2.3

I would like to reiterate here in the security considerations

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
Minutes meeting 2023-01-19:

* Andreas: normative statement should be in EC section
* Aron: have no such section
* Stavros: section 1
* Falko: sec 1 not normative
* Aron: move normative statement to sec 1
* Aron and Falko: make new section 2 with normative preliminaries

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body: Adressed in https://github.com/openpgp-pqc/draft-openpgp-pqc/pull/21

issues.title: Consistency with respect to wording "extraction" of component key
issues.author: falko-strenzke
issues.body:
# Falko's original remark

In Section 4.1.2 we state

> Extract the component public key kyberPublicKey that is part of the recipient's composite public key

This seems inconsistent in wording with the descriptions for the EC-KEMs, where no mentioning of the extraction of the component public key from the composite key is made. IMO it should be mentioned in for each scheme or for none.

# Aron's comment on this (from previous issue)

> In Section 4.1.2
>This seems inconsistent in wording with the descriptions for the EC-KEMs, 

In the ECC sections we mention 
```...where R is the component public key eccPublicKey...```
I would consider removing point (1) from 4.1.2 and rewrite
``` 
2. Invoke (kyberCipherText, keyShare) <- kyberKem.encap(kyberPublicKey) 
   where kyberPublicKey is extracted from recipient's composite public key
```

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
Meeting 2023-01-19:

* Aron & Falko: just about consistency, will be aligned

issues.title: From pqc@ietf: Summary OpenPGP crypto functions and further references
issues.author: fluppe2
issues.body:
In view of

https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/pqc/rX2AcijHUbdiijehEaZS5PmpulQ/

address (how?) the following

>> I think it would also benefit from a intro section that summarizes OpenPGP Crypto functions. ... Even references to the proper RFCs for more info would be beneficial.

> This RFC is designed to be an addition to the crypto-refresh (what the WG is working on right now), and is directly based on it. I think you're right about the references, and it could be very beneficial to add more links to it, with specific references to the sections.

comments.author: wussler
comments.body: Personally I think there are still some places where we can add backreferences, especially when we introduce packets or so on. This is mostly cosmetic, and I can take care of this.

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
I think we can orient towards RFC 6637 in this respect. [There](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6637#section-9), it is written for instance

```
9. Encoding of Public and Private Keys

   The following algorithm-specific packets are added to Section 5.5.2
   of [RFC4880], "Public-Key Packet Formats", to support ECDH and ECDSA.
```

It also [mentions](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6637#section-10) where no changes are needed:
```
10.  Message Encoding with Public Keys

   Section 5.2.2 of [RFC4880], "Version 3 Signature Packet Format"
   defines signature formats.  No changes in the format are needed for
   ECDSA.
```

issues.title: Should we point to https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ounsworth-cfrg-kem-combiners/
issues.author: fluppe2
issues.body: Such that our proposal for KEM combination automatically docks to the discussion of this draft?

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
Minutes Meeting 2023-01-19:

* Stavros: expect to be discussed in CFRG and our construction is an instance of this
* Aron: wait until CFRG has given a vote
* Andreas: what ounsworth needs is a dual-prf, but we use SHA3 which is thus a dual-prf
* Andreas: the simple way is to just concat everything into a KDF, but this is not addresing the attacks that were pointed out some time ago. 
* Stavros: we can decide whether to include reference
* Andreas, Stavros: when CFRG has voted, add statement to sec. considerations

pulls.title: Change KDF domain separation to be > 32 chars
pulls.author: wussler
pulls.body:
NIST.SP.800-185-2 specifies:
> Applications of this Recommendation shall not select an input key, K, whose length is less than their required security strength. Guidance for cryptographic algorithm and key-size selection is available in [4].

I would raise the length of our domain separation string to > 32 chars for conformance with the specification. Also many implementations implement this strictly, so this ensures easy reuse of existing implementations.

Furthermore I'd drop the `V5` from the string. I don't think we want to customize the string based on the PKESK version (we also don't for ECDH, that can be used across v4 and v5)

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body: I'm not sure I understand. It is the KMAC *key* that needs to have a sufficient length. The domain seperation just needs to be unique.

comments.author: wussler
comments.body: As far as I understand we're effectively using `domSeparation` as a KMAC key

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body: OK, I see, it really seems to be an sublte inconsistency between the NIST specification of the one step key deriviation based on KMAC and this restriction of KMAC regarding the key length. So I think your suggestion is the correct solution.

comments.author: fluppe2
comments.body:
We can extend the length of the domain seperation string, but I wouldn't put "Kyber" in there for two reasons:

a) The name "Kyber" will eventually turn into "MLWE". I suggest to keep the amount of necessary code changes when we align to the forthcoming NIST standards as small as possible. This is somehow an analoguous argument as to not include version information.

b) The composite KDF does not care if it is feeded with ECC, Kyber or something else. Therefore I suggest to keep the domain seperation generic. e.g. if the CFRG decides to standardize NTRU in the future, the KDF will receive maybe an ECC || NTRU value.

I would suggest, instead of putting "OpenPGPKyberCompositeKeyDerivation" we should do something like "OpenPGPCompositeKeyDerivationFunction".

comments.author: wussler
comments.body:
So @fluppe2 you would keep the same hash for Kyber or other algorithms?

I am happy with the proposal `OpenPGPCompositeKeyDerivationFunction`

comments.author: fluppe2
comments.body: @wussler : Yes, I would prefer the domain separation to specify a generic composer, so that potential future PQC-extensions for OpenPGP could adopt this. The fixedInfo field contains the specific algorithm information anyway.

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
> @wussler : Yes, I would prefer the domain separation to specify a generic composer, so that potential future PQC-extensions for OpenPGP could adopt this. The fixedInfo field contains the specific algorithm information anyway.

Makes sense, is fine for me, too

pulls.title: Add parameter IDs condition for SPHINCS+
pulls.author: wussler
pulls.body: When issuing or verifying SPHINCS+ signature, it is not specified how to handle parameter IDs

pulls.title: Changed value of 'domSeparation' to the UTF-8 encoding of 'OpenPGPCom…
pulls.author: fluppe2

pulls.title: Change sentence for SHA3 support requirement
pulls.author: wussler

pulls.title: New year review falko
pulls.author: falko-strenzke
pulls.body: Most changes were previously discussed in https://github.com/openpgp-pqc/draft-openpgp-pqc/issues/6. The respective comments are indicated by crossed out text in the issue.

comments.body: I think the 'a' is not necessary here, because the capacity is a definite value. But I might be mistaken.
I think the part about previous specs is not necessary here, as it is already said in §1.3.1. The formulations here should be brief. I liked the old sentence better.
Now the list seems to go from 1 then 3,4,5. Is the 2 missing?

```suggestion
 1. Generate an ephemeral key pair {`v`, `V=vG`} according to
    the procedure defined in [RFC7748], that is, without any
    measures such as masking or clamping
```

```suggestion
 4. Encode the EdDSA and Dilithium signatures according to the packet
    structure given in {{ecc-dilithium-sig-packet}}.
```

```suggestion
 4. Encode the ECDSA and Dilithium signatures according to the packet
    structure given in {{ecc-dilithium-sig-packet}}.
```

```suggestion
In order not to extend the attack surface, we bind the hash algorithm used for
message digestion to the hash algorithm used internally by the signature
algorithm. Dilithium internally uses a SHAKE256 digest, therefore we require
SHA3 in the Dilithium + ECC signature packet. In the case of SPHINCS+ the
internal hash algorithm varies based on the algorithm and parameter ID.
```
Probably it's better to remove the reference to older OpenPGP specs, but i like the sentence either way
Also to me it "sounds better" with the `a` but I see Stavros' point
FYI this does not matter with markdown, but I agree it's nice to keep tidy

```suggestion
More precisely, for given capacity `c` the indifferentiability proof shows that
```
I don't know english grammar well enough to be sure but also I don't mind too much. Maybe we get a review from an english native speaker at some point.
For me introducing an 'a' is fine.

pulls.title: Update Key Share size for Kyber
pulls.author: wussler
pulls.body: As defined in section 1.4 of the spec, Kyber.CCAKEM outputs a `sha-256`, therefore it's always 32 octets long

comments.body:
```suggestion
The SPHINCS+ key generation is performed according to the function `spx_keygen()` specified in
```
There was something missing like "the function is used" in the previous sentence.

```suggestion
component keys. Dilithium key generation follows the specification in
```
Aligned to the text for Kyber, fixed typo.

reviews.author: fluppe2
reviews.body: Apart from the suggestions great for me.

pulls.title: Add some guidance to prevent parallel encryption
pulls.author: wussler

comments.body:
This seems to refer to this proposal: https://gitlab.com/openpgp-wg/rfc4880bis/-/issues/64

But this is not yet part of the crypto-refresh. So I would prefer not to include it here until it becomes adapted into the cr-re
How is this check to performed exactly? By comparing the user IDs of the keys?
Is there a 'the' missing?, i.e. should it read 'from the protocol perspective'?
Indeed, added the missing 'the'.

pulls.title: minor additions in kem-combiner section
pulls.author: falko-strenzke
pulls.body: Since we give an explicit description of the one-step key derivation in terms of KMAC, a reference to the defintion of KMAC is also necessary in my view.

comments.body:
```suggestion
    target: https://doi.org/10.6028/NIST.SP.800-185
```

```suggestion
    title: SHA-3 Derived Functions: cSHAKE, KMAC, TupleHash, and ParallelHash
```

```suggestion
{{NIST-SP800-56C}} Section 4, based on KMAC256
```
The DOI is a much more future-proof target.
Aligned to the other NIST citations (without repeating "NIST Special...") we have, and aligned to the original title.
Smoothed text a little and inserted a comma for easier reading.

reviews.author: fluppe2
reviews.body: Agreed that a reference to KMAC256 is helpful. Added some suggestions.

comments.body: "NIST Special Publication 800-185" is the essential part of the title, without it the reference is formally not complete (and be just guessed via our label, which is not sufficient and overly lengthy due to holding the whole string, I would say) omitting it is in my opinion out of the question. I make a different proposal though, following how the crypto-refresh handles this.
Ok, I'm fine with this. Then we should also align the other reference to NIST SP 800 56C, so that it is homogeneous.
I think this still has to be addressed.

pulls.title: aligned NIST references to the crypto-refresh
pulls.author: falko-strenzke

comments.body:
```suggestion
      NIST Special Publication 800-186
```

"SP" not needed if spelled out.

Aligned to the above proposal for the other reference SP800-56A.
